metadata.seed: Well, I'm working full time. I'm a part time student. I've just gotten engaged, and I live at home. There's just so much stress in my life. I have everything happening all at once. Just to top it all off, I failed an exam, a really big exam, and I just feel like I can't do anything anymore. I can't do anything right. I've just got so much stress, and I feel like such a disappointment, such a failure. And I've embarrassed my family by failing. I just don't know what to do anymore. I guess I've come here today to tell you, like, I want to leave uni, and I guess that's why I've come here to you today.

patient.query: Well, I'm working full time. I'm a part time student. I've just gotten engaged, and I live at home. There's just so much stress in my life. I have everything happening all at once. Just to top it all off, I failed an exam, a really big exam, and I just feel like I can't do anything anymore. I can't do anything right. I've just got so much stress, and I feel like such a disappointment, such a failure. And I've embarrassed my family by failing. I just don't know what to do anymore. I guess I've come here today to tell you, like, I want to leave uni, and I guess that's why I've come here to you today.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re juggling a lot—work, school, a new engagement, and a big exam failure—and feeling overwhelmed and disappointed. What do you think might help you feel a bit more in control right now?

safety_check.response: It sounds like you’re feeling overwhelmed and disappointed by the exam failure and the many responsibilities you’re juggling. What small step do you think could help you feel a bit more in control right now?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s just… a lot. I feel like I’m constantly trying to keep everything balanced, but I’m just not sure I’m doing it very well. Sometimes I think if I could just manage my time better or get a little more structure, maybe that would help? But then I get stuck, and it feels like I’m just... spinning.

if I could just manage my time better
if I could get a little more structure

patient.query: Well, I mean, there have been times when I’ve tried to do that, and it felt a bit easier, I guess? But then there are other moments when I end up overwhelmed anyway, and it just feels like I’m going in circles. I can’t quite shake the thought that maybe I’m just not capable of sticking to it.

triggers: times when I’ve tried to do that
moments when I end up overwhelmed
automatic_thoughts: I’m just not capable of sticking to it

patient.query: I remember there was a time when I started making a list of little tasks each day. It felt nice checking them off. But then I’d miss a day or two, and it would feel like, what was the point? It’s just frustrating because I want to feel like I’m making progress, but it all feels so fragile.

llm_response.response: It sounds like you felt a boost of accomplishment when you could check off tasks, but missing a day makes you question the point and feel frustrated. What might help you feel more resilient when a day slips?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you felt a boost of accomplishment when you could check off tasks, but missing a day makes you question the point and feel frustrated. What might help you feel more resilient when a day slips?

socratic_questioning.response: What evidence do you have that missing a day of tasks, which can happen when routines slip, makes the whole effort pointless?

alternative_perspective.response: It can feel fragile when a day slips by, but a friend might point out that each completed task still counts toward progress. What might a supportive friend say about the value of the progress you’ve already made?

safety_check.response: It sounds like you’re feeling frustrated and uncertain about the value of your daily tasks. What do you think might help you feel more secure about the progress you’re making?

patient.query: I remember a time when I made a list of little tasks each day. It felt nice checking things off. But then I’d miss a day or two, and it would just feel like, what was the point? It’s frustrating because I really want to feel like I’m making progress, but everything feels so fragile.

triggers: missing a day or two of tasks
automatic_thoughts: I want to feel like I'm making progress

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you’re feeling pretty frustrated when a day slips by and the list feels pointless. That frustration is understandable—when you’re trying to build momentum, a missed day can feel like a setback that makes everything feel fragile.

What do you think happens to your sense of progress when you miss a day? Are there moments when you’ve missed a day but still felt you were moving forward? What evidence do you have that the list is truly “pointless” when a day is missed? Sometimes looking at the bigger picture can help us see that a single day doesn’t erase the gains you’ve made on the days you did check things off.

If you’re open to it, you might try thinking about what a supportive friend would say to you in this situation. They might point out that missing a day doesn’t negate the progress you’ve already made, and that the list can still be a useful tool if it’s adjusted to fit your schedule. How could you tweak the list or your routine so that it feels more resilient to a day or two of slip‑ups?

validate_and_reflect.response: It sounds like you’re feeling frustrated when a day slips and you wonder if the effort is worth it, especially because you’re eager to see progress and everything feels fragile. What might make the next day feel more manageable or meaningful for you?

socratic_questioning.response: What evidence do you have that missing a day or two makes the whole effort feel pointless?

alternative_perspective.response: If a close friend were watching you, what might they say about the value of those lists? You might find that even small, flexible steps can still feel like progress.

safety_check.response: It sounds like missing a day or two makes you feel like the whole effort is pointless and everything feels fragile. What might help you feel more secure about your progress even when things slip?

patient.query: Staying on top of things... I guess it looks like having a routine that I can stick to without feeling like I’m juggling too much. Like, when I wake up, I want to have a clear idea of what my day will look like and feel confident about it. But then, when I'm not able to do that, it’s like I just spiral down into feeling overwhelmed and lost, which makes me doubt if I can really handle it.

neutral: Personality trait typical of long term functioning (finding)

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, maybe starting with just a simple morning ritual could help? Like, just taking a few minutes to have a cup of coffee and breathe before diving into my day. But then I worry that even that could turn into another thing I might mess up, and it makes me hesitate. I want to feel good about it, but sometimes it feels like I’m just setting myself up for failure.
